A comprehensive analysis of professional athletes indicates that rigorous physical training optimization correlates with longevity. Although the relationship between aerobic and anaerobic fitness has received little attention, the potential for athletes to reduce or eliminate chronic disease through the reduction of chronic disease risk factors for athletes is evident. A strength of this review is that it summarizes evidence suggesting a link between training-associated changes in aerobic fitness and mortality. Although not all studies have been conducted with athletes, previous studies with nonathletes have shown some similar associations between training and decreased mortality [9,10]. In view of the importance of exercise in the prevention of chronic disease and aging-related processes, the aim of exercise is to increase body weight, fat mass, and fat-free mass (FFLM). The primary focus of this review is to summarize and integrate the evidence to explain that the training-related changes in body composition appear to be associated with improvements in performance (i.e., health) and health span. The psychological impact of home-stadium cheering significantly alters the performance metrics of competing teams. During the 2011 season, there were significant, sustained home-court advantages for teams that received a home-court advantage during play (i.e., the team that scored more points per game) relative to those with a lower home-court advantage during play (e.g., the team that conceded more points per game) (13). For example, an advantage of 9.3 points per game was obtained when one team received home-court advantage during play compared to when the other team did (i.e., a 50% advantage). In some instances, a home-court advantage of 4.7 points per contest was observed, suggesting that a home-court advantage of 11.3 points will result in a net advantage of approximately 10 points for the home team in the match.

The devotion of dedicated fans often serves as a quantifiable variable in franchise revenue and team morale. The New York Yankees earned more than $220 million from their loyal, dedicated supporters in 2012. Similarly, the Seattle Mariners, who have historically struggled to attract fans of this ilk, have a fan base that spends an average of $14.50 per year. The Red Sox are among the best at selling tickets, however, with the average Red Sox fan spending a total of $36.46 in 2013. For perspective, that is almost half as much as the Yankees fan spends a year.

A formal evaluation of defensive strategies in professional football highlights the evolution of modern coaching.

As teams prepare for the 2013 playoffs, they are trying to replicate a formula that got them to the conference championship game. The key to success, according to the study, is a defense that plays in an organized, disciplined fashion.

"Defensive coaches will say they've tried everything," said the study's author, Greg Gant, an assistant professor in the university's communication and journalism department. "And the one thing they'll never try is organized chaos."

As coaches prepare for the first postseason game, the study, which surveyed coaches and defensive coordinators across the league, highlights how much offensive success can be reduced by using the right strategy.

Gant said part of the goal of the study was to get a better handle on coaches' reactions to each game they play. They often don't have a chance to evaluate what they've done in each game until after the fact, Gant said.

Elite athletes must maintain strict physical regimens to sustain peak competitive advantages throughout the grueling season. These practices include an almost constant physical conditioning program, including regular strenuous exercise (e.g. lifting heavy objects, running, walking, cycling), flexibility training and stress management, to name a few. Additionally, players receive advanced training to optimize their immune function, muscle, bone and joint health, nutritional health, psychological well-being, and performance.

Despite these rigorous, consistent training regimes, elite athletes are at a disadvantage to everyday non-athletes; even in the same sport. This is because they are not only working harder as a group, they are also more fit.

Athlete physical condition – compared to their fitness level - is often called "training load," and can be measured with a simple questionnaire that asks respondents to weigh their current fitness level on scales ranging from very fit (where there is "enough energy" but little pain) to very unfit (where there is "too much pain" but little energy). So I was walking through the woods at night, and out of nowhere, this weird glowing portal just opened up... And, I walked into it with my flashlight and my headlight too. I had no idea what I was walking into, but I stepped inside and I started hearing a lot of weird noises. These aren't just little random noises though, these really weird sounds... It started out with one sound. Something weird, like a very loud, high-pitched sound that was clearly high pitch for a human, but I couldn't say whether it was natural or synthetic... A long and very faint and incredibly high-pitched buzzing sound from inside me. When I started hearing it, I decided that I would not be walking into an open portal. I got to my feet and started walking back out. I walked for a while, and I started to notice that the only way to get out of that place was to leave it or to have it be open to the world. I was being carried through the portal, but I was not getting further and was getting closer and closer to the portal.

I bought a vintage mirror at a flea market, but the reflection inside isn't moving when I move. Is this a bug or a feature? I've also found that the mirror is not reflecting when I lean against it (or hold something in front of it, or lean forward, etc.) — a common feature in "mirror images."

Advertisement

In general, the old style glass used more mirror surface area in a single surface. This led to more reflections and in a modern light, that is pretty much what the mirror looks like. The reflective surface in the glass is actually the glass itself that is reflecting. This is why in the old style mirrors, you'll typically see more "tangles" and reflections in the glass from a particular direction. The reason for this is that the glass contains two layers: a mirror top, and a reflective layer. The layers of glass are designed to reflect light back to a point, without reflection or "fuzz."

Why do I still have glass mirrors to see through now that all the newer mirrors no longer have this feature?

A comparative study of modern crossovers reveals distinct advantages in terms of fuel efficiency and safety ratings.

The research is a collaboration between the European Automobile Manufacturers' Association and the European Commission Joint Research Centre (JRC) in Potsdam, Germany.

'Crossovers are the world's most fuel-efficient transport modes, with vehicles producing less CO2 and the atmosphere in general, when compared to internal vehicles' explains Stefan Bostrom from the Potsdam JRC.

The study analysed 20 different vehicles and the associated fuel economy and CO2 emissions.

"The results show that Crossovers have an enormous advantage as compared to both internal and hybrid cars. The results will help manufacturers and governments to choose which models are most suitable for different situations," says Martin Bracke of EAM. "Many more of us will travel in Crossovers than in internal vehicles in the future."

This study describes the relationship between Cd and Cpl as well as the impact of design features such as aerodynamic strakes, air-intake and air-exhaust openings, and wing shape on their respective values. Analysis also focuses on some of the variables, such as the effect of a single, large strake (e.g., a "V-shaped strake") on the Cd and Cpl, and the consequences of the effects of different strakes on other parameters, such as peak lift and wing area. Ocean warming and the resultant reduction in sea ice melt, in turn, can have significant impacts on the biosphere. Sea-ice reduction also has a direct effect on climate and atmospheric circulation on land. Sea-ice loss has the potential to amplify existing temperature and rainfall patterns, which in turn have significant impacts on terrestrial ecosystems and global food security. These impacts will likely increase after 2100.

To ensure that the human beings of Earth benefit from this ongoing shift to a CO2-dominated climate, we must accelerate efforts to phase out fossil fuel emissions and develop low-carbon energy sources that can provide the world with the essential economic mobility, energy security, and resilience to climate change needs.

Policy analysts evaluate the efficacy of carbon taxing as a mechanism to curb industrial pollutant output. A single tax on a unit of CO 2 emissions can be used as a mechanism to reduce industrial pollutants at a rate sufficient to offset reductions from the implementation of regulations. The most prominent carbon accounting method for assessing the efficacy of carbon taxes is the revenue-cycle analysis. This approach is designed to capture how a tax revenue varies over time. This paper argues that the revenue-cycle analysis is only a theoretical construct and that it does not consider the economic implications of an increased carbon tax. In the absence of a direct relationship between the tax rate and the quantity of pollution that is taxed, one can only measure efficiency effects. Accounting for this effect is the subject of this paper. There is a wealth of literature demonstrating that carbon taxes are efficient mechanisms to combat CO 2 emissions. This paper focuses on a key issue with the revenue-cycle analysis: why does carbon pricing tend to be politically controversial? The paper concludes with an assessment of the implications of this controversy for future policy planning.

The neurobiological basis of memory consolidation requires structured intervals of study and adequate rest. Longer intervals can facilitate retrieval of memories, whereas shorter intervals can be associated with failure to recall previously acquired information. This paper describes the effects of sleep deprivation on the ability to maintain short-term memory for short intervals of time. Four experiments involving nine participants involved either four or eight hours of sleep deprivation separated by a 6-hour day during which subjects performed two tasks that typically required a high degree of concentration. Subjects performed the two tasks after a 14-hour period of sleep, prior to sleep onset, and after a 1-hour nap. Subjects performed significantly worse at learning a new sequence than after sleep deprivation. After sleep deprivation, the ability to acquire a sequence took significantly longer to be maintained than after a 1-hour nap. There were no significant differences in any of the other retention measures. The findings demonstrate that sleep deprivation has a significant negative effect on retention of information learned over a relatively short interval of time.

For example, the time investment into a book, the amount of reading per day or time spent per week has no statistical association with how much your grades changed over time. This is the most straightforward example of the limitations of schedule-based evaluation with respect to measuring academic ability. On the other hand, there is reason to think that having a dedicated study schedule does result in different work patterns and a greater focus on the particular tasks at hand. The evolution of cinematography in modern media reflects significant technological advancements in digital rendering. Cinematography is no exception to this dynamic new frontier.

What is anamorphic digital imaging?

Anamorphic cinema is an approach to filmmaking used to shoot film with a large format lens while avoiding distortion of image by using two or more lenses. The image is projected with two lenses, one in every eye, simultaneously, in order to preserve visual fidelity. The image is then projected on the subject(s) via a second, smaller (anamorphic) lens. The image of the first set of lenses is then combined with one of the subject's eyes to create the three-dimensional image.

Where did anamorphic digital imaging first come from?

Anamorphic digital imaging dates back to 1948 when it became well recognized that the image projected through two smaller lenses, such as those often used by cinematographers, produced images with less clarity.

A comprehensive study of justice system reforms examines the socioeconomic impacts of mandatory minimum sentences. Over 30 separate studies from around the world are featured, examining the overall effects of mandatory criminal justice sentences on crime victimization, family formation, crime deterrence, incarceration, and economic opportunities; and the overall effects of reducing the severity of these mandatory sentences on victimizations and other outcomes. More than a decade of research on the issue is cited, and more than two dozen policy recommendations, some of which were developed in the 1990s in the United States, are introduced. Drawing on a wide range of research on criminal justice, this report finds that the majority of research shows that mandatory minimum sentences do not serve to reduce crime or reduce crime victimizations.

Introduction

Mandatory minimum sentences are among the most controversial aspects of U.S. criminal justice policy. While not the subject of much public debate, mandatory minimums have long-lasting effects and have contributed to a growing disparity between the United States and other countries.

Criminologists evaluate the correlation between community policing initiatives and public trust in urban areas. For example, a review of eight metropolitan police departments with high rates of community policing found that trust in police fell in cities where a large percentage of the force is dedicated to community policing and in cities where officers worked closely with community leaders. Community policing efforts can help enhance public trust, but community policing is primarily a response to crime rates, and crime must be contained and decreased to sustain trust in government. Research indicates that community policing initiatives may be most effective at reducing violence when implemented in high-risk areas. Research also shows that community policing is associated with decreases in arrest rates and increases in the arrest of repeat and petty offenders, but less consistent with the claim that arrests are related to community policing. In cities that have adopted policies that encourage community policing, there has been no association between increased community policing and an increase in the overall number of arrests over time. The economic consequences of trade tariff implementation remain a central point of debate among prominent analysts. Although empirical data on tariff implementation is still lacking, the presence of trade surcharges, which are used by a considerable majority of countries against imports from developing countries, suggests that they have had effects beyond simply raising import prices.

As a consequence of tariff measures, imports from developing countries are in general much more expensive than similar imports from advanced economies, due to the inclusion of various additional duties on imported inputs, like steel. The prices of these goods are thus higher in the developing countries. The trade deficit associated with the imposition of high tariffs may also contribute to high prices for the country's export product (e.g. consumer goods). The introduction of protectionist measures against the imported goods, and consequently of high prices, may reduce economic activity in developing countries and thereby contribute to an increasing trade deficit.

The intersection of political rhetoric and market stability often results in localized equity market fluctuations. Local price differentials between stocks are a relatively new phenomenon that can quickly be transformed into global differences of several percentage points in a short period of time. Market valuations are often based on the perceptions of market participants about the state of the local economic business climate. Equity markets may be particularly susceptible to market fluctuations induced by social and political factors if it is considered that markets react to social and political influences on economic variables.

In this article, we demonstrate through analysis of historical stock market movements that stock pricing changes based on an individual's perceived socioeconomic position can be influenced by the state of the local business environments (LBE's). From 1980-1996, we show that in order for a market valuation to be based on a market indicator, a market component must be specified that reflects the economic conditions of the economy.
